findings.issue: The spec says the --show-memory SELECT is issued by the loop goroutine 'on its report cadence', but the loop goroutine has no ticker of its own — it is driven by rate.Limiter.Allow(). The mechanism for triggering a timed memory read from inside the rate-limited loop is unspecified: if the implementation uses time.Since(lastReport) >= ReportInterval as an inline check after each Allow() iteration, the cadence will be approximate and PREPARE-count-dependent at high rates. This is acceptable for a diagnostic tool, but the ambiguity may lead an implementor to add a second ticker in the loop goroutine — which would be fine architecturally — or to skip the timing and read on every N-th iteration, breaking the cadence semantics. A one-line note in Decision 6 describing the recommended timing mechanism (e.g. 'use time.Since check inside the loop after each Exec') would close this gap.
findings.fix: Add to Decision 6: 'The loop goroutine performs the memory SELECT by checking time.Since(lastMemoryRead) >= ReportInterval after each Conn.Exec call, then updating the atomic and resetting the timestamp. No separate ticker is needed in the loop goroutine.'

summary: All three round-1 findings are correctly addressed. The counter==0 init-error guard (Decision 5) cleanly separates builder bugs from OOM events, consistent with rollbacks.runWorker conventions. The separate ticker goroutine reading an atomic counter is sound Go concurrency practice — pgx.Conn is not goroutine-safe, and the spec enforces the invariant that only the loop goroutine calls Conn.Exec/Query. One minor implementation ambiguity remains: how the loop goroutine times its --show-memory SELECT without its own ticker. Not a blocker; easily resolved during implementation.
